food_name: Lemongrass Braised Chicken with Steamed Rice
aliases: Lemongrass Braised Chicken with Steamed Rice
Braised lemongrass chili chicken with rice

english: Lemongrass Braised Chicken with Steamed Rice
Braised lemongrass chili chicken with rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese lemongrass braised chicken with rice; 'chicken dish' (FOODON_00002809) used as the closest defensible broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Vietnamese kho-style braised chicken with rice. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is used as the primary ingredient anchor. The rice component is not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps specifically to this Vietnamese composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of approximately 550 g, comprising a portion of braised lemongrass chili chicken alongside a mound of steamed white rice, as typically served at Vietnamese casual dining restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia and Southeast Asia — composite dish estimation using chicken (cooked) and steamed white rice components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the exact composite dish 'Cơm gà kho sả ớt'. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data for cooked chicken (skin-on, braised) and steamed white rice in an approximate 40:60 chicken-to-rice ratio by weight, adjusted for fish sauce and lemongrass seasoning contribution to sodium. Input per_100g values (energy 118.18 kcal, protein 8.18 g, fat 4.55 g, carbohydrate 11.82 g) are consistent with this ratio and are retained as the working estimate. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, stewed' (NDB 05013) and 'Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked' (NDB 20045) were used as cross-validation anchors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (cooked, long-grain)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite dish 'Cơm gà kho sả ớt'. GI estimated at 64 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (long-grain, GI ~64 per University of Sydney GI Database). The braised chicken component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the dish GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (64 × 65.01) / 100 ≈ 41.6, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 11.82 × 5.5 = 65.01 g, rounded to 41.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 218 mg/100 g; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce in the braising liquid, avoid adding condiments at the table, and limit the serving to half a plate to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~41.5) driven by steamed white rice (GI 64); low dietary fibre (0.55 g/100 g) accelerates postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute steamed white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, reduce the rice portion to no more than half a cup cooked, and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.27 g/100 g and cholesterol 32.73 mg/100 g are moderate; sodium at 218 mg/100 g contributes to cardiovascular risk at full serving size.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken pieces to reduce saturated fat, request minimal fish sauce to lower sodium, and limit the serving size to control total saturated fat and sodium intake per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lemongrass (citral, polyphenols) and garlic (allicin) provide anti-inflammatory compounds; chicken is lean poultry with low arachidonic acid content.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory properties of lemongrass and garlic, but should monitor chili intake as a nightshade ingredient and request reduced fish sauce to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cơm gà kho sả ớt (Lemongrass Braised Chicken with Steamed Rice) is a classic Vietnamese home-style and restaurant dish consisting of chicken pieces slow-braised using the 'kho' technique — a Vietnamese caramelised braising method — in a savoury-sweet sauce built from fish sauce, sugar, lemongrass, chili, garlic, and shallots, served alongside a generous portion of steamed long-grain white rice. The chicken is tender and deeply flavoured, with the lemongrass imparting a bright citrus-herbal aroma and the chili providing mild to moderate heat. The braising sauce reduces to a glossy, concentrated coating on the chicken. Nutritionally, a standard 550 g plate delivers approximately 650 kcal, 45 g protein, 25 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate, with sodium around 1200 mg per serving. The dish has a medium glycemic index (~64) driven by the white rice component and a high glycemic load (~41.5) per serving, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Saturated fat is moderate at 1.27 g/100 g. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets. It is a staple of Vietnamese cuisine found across Vietnam and in Vietnamese restaurants throughout Southeast Asia, including Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite Vietnamese dish. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data for cooked chicken and steamed white rice, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries. The input per_100g values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with the component-based estimate and are retained. GI is estimated by analogy from the dominant carbohydrate source (white rice, GI 64, University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese braised chicken and rice dish are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA tables or USDA FoodData Central as a composite entry; component-level data would require weighted recipe reconstruction beyond the scope of this record. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.02 g/100 g from the input source; this is a trace level consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in poultry and is retained. 3. Nutrient values are estimated from component data (FAO/INFOODS SEA + USDA SR Legacy cross-validation) and should be treated as working estimates pending direct laboratory analysis of the dish. 4. The dish is sold on GrabFood Singapore under the name 'Cơm gà kho sả ớt'; country_of_origin in the input is listed as Singapore (point of sale), but the cuisine origin is Vietnam. 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation with chicken and fish sauce; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 6. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on steamed long-grain white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources.
